Your employer's policies

Birthing parents can take pre-birth disability leave with a doctor's note. This leave is job-protected and may be covered under your Short-Term Disability plan at 60% pay.
Birthing parents can take 6 weeks of disability for a vaginal delivery and 8 weeks of disability for a C-section delivery. This runs concurrently with FMLA and is paid at 60% through STD, topped up to 100% by your employer for the first 6 weeks.
All new parents are entitled to up to 12 weeks of bonding time. This leave is job-protected under FMLA. Your employer provides bonding time as unpaid, but it may run concurrently with any remaining FMLA entitlement.
What happens next
Your absence request is pending. Here's what to expect:
1
Eligibility check
We're verifying your eligibility for each benefit. This typically takes 3–5 business days.
2
Documentation
You may be asked to upload medical certification or other supporting documents.
3
Approval & confirmation
Once approved, you'll receive a confirmation with your finalized leave dates and pay details.

Pregnancy Absence

DENIED
Case Number: LV-2026-8472
Your absence request was not approved
Review the details to understand why. You may have options to provide more information or explore other benefits.
REQUESTED START
March 16, 2026
REQUESTED END
June 30, 2026
REQUESTED RETURN
July 1, 2026
DURATION
107 days

Reason for Denial

Insufficient Documentation

The medical certification provided does not meet the requirements for absence approval. Required documentation must include:

  • Complete medical certification from healthcare provider
  • Expected delivery date verification
  • Medical necessity statement for the requested duration
Eligibility Requirements Not Met

Based on our records, you have not met the minimum service requirement of 12 months and 1,250 hours worked in the past year for FMLA eligibility.

Absence Management

Taking time off
shouldn't be stressful

We'll guide you through every step — from planning your absence to understanding your pay and getting support.

Planning
Claim
Pay
Support
You'll be able to review everything before submitting.

Select Reason For Planned Absence

Select the primary reason for your planned absence.

Employee's Own Illness or Injury
Care for sick Family Member with Health Condition
Non-Birthing Parent, Adoption, or Foster Care Placement (Bonding)
Birthing Parent, Pregnancy, or Bonding
Military Related Activity
Other
This helps us identify which benefits and protections may apply to you.
Save as draft and come back later

Planned New Child Details

Here you can provide some details about your new child to plan your absence.

Child has already arrived
The birth has already occurred
Planning for a future arrival
You're requesting absence in anticipation
Your best estimate is fine — this can be updated later.
Some entitlements may be shared between spouses employed by the same company.
These details help us estimate your timeline and coverage.
Save as draft and come back later

Employment information

Provide your tax withholding state and your start date with your employer. This helps us suggest state benefits you may qualify for.

We’ll use this to check for state-specific benefits you may qualify for.
Save as draft and come back later

Is this related to your job?

This helps us determine if your absence may be covered under workers' compensation.

Yes, it's job-related
The illness or injury occurred at work or was caused by work duties
No, it's not job-related
The illness or injury is unrelated to your work
This may qualify for Workers' Compensation

Job-related injuries and illnesses are typically covered under your employer's workers' compensation program, which provides separate benefits for medical expenses and lost wages.

Recommended next steps:
  • Report the incident to your supervisor or HR
  • File an incident report with your employer
  • Contact your workers' comp administrator

You can still continue with this leave request. Some job-related absences may qualify for both workers' comp and leave benefits.

Tell us about your situation

This helps us estimate your coverage and benefits. We won't ask for medical details until after you've designed your plan.

New condition
A recent illness, injury, or surgery
Ongoing condition
A chronic or recurring condition that requires time away
Fully unable to work
You'll need continuous time off for treatment or recovery
Working intermittently
You'll need periodic time off for flare-ups or appointments
Working reduced hours
You can work but need a lighter schedule during treatment
These details help us estimate your timeline and coverage. You'll provide medical details later.

Tell us about your caregiving situation

This helps us estimate your coverage and benefits. We won't ask for medical details until after you've designed your plan.

Short-term
Recovery from surgery, acute illness, or a defined treatment period
Ongoing
Chronic condition that requires recurring time away to provide care
Continuous
A single block of time off to provide full-time care
Intermittent
Periodic absences for appointments, treatments, or flare-ups
Reduced schedule
Shorter work days or fewer days per week to provide care
These details help us estimate your coverage. You'll provide the family member's medical details later.

Your employer's policies

If you are enrolled, STD provides income replacement at 60% of your salary for up to 26 weeks after a 7-day waiting period. Medical certification is required.
FMLA provides up to 12 weeks of job-protected, unpaid absence per year for a serious health condition. Your position (or equivalent) is guaranteed upon return.
If you need additional time beyond FMLA, your employer may grant extended leave as a reasonable accommodation on a case-by-case basis. This leave is unpaid and not job-protected by law.

FMLA provides up to 12 weeks of job-protected, unpaid absence per year to care for a spouse, child, or parent with a serious health condition. Medical certification from the family member's provider is required.

Your Estimated Eligibility

Based on your situation, employment history, and work state, here's what you may qualify for.

FMLA (Job Protection)
Eligible
Up to 12 weeks of unpaid, job-protected absence. Your position (or equivalent) is guaranteed upon return.
Based on: 6+ years tenure, 1,250+ hours worked
Company Paid Parental Leave
Eligible
6 weeks at 100% pay for birthing parents. Runs concurrently with FMLA.
Based on: Full-time employee, 1+ year tenure
Short-Term Disability (STD)
Eligible
Up to 8 weeks at 60% of your salary for the medical recovery period after delivery.
Based on: Enrolled in employer STD plan
State Paid Family Leave
Not Available
Missouri does not currently offer a state paid family leave program.
Based on: Work state MO

Your Personalized To-Do List

Here's what you'll need to do before and during your absence. We'll guide you through each step.

Before Your Absence

Submit your formal absence request
File your official request so your employer can begin processing your absence and coordinating coverage.
Recommended: 30+ days before absence
Get medical certification from your provider
Your healthcare provider needs to complete a medical certification form confirming your pregnancy and expected due date.
Required for FMLA & STD
Discuss your absence plan with your manager
Coordinate a transition plan, including coverage for your responsibilities and any handoff documentation.
Recommended: 2+ weeks before absence

During Your Absence

File your STD claim after delivery
Once your child arrives, submit your Short-Term Disability claim to begin receiving 60% pay benefits.
Within 7 days of delivery
Upload proof of birth for bonding benefits
A birth certificate or hospital record is needed to activate your company parental bonding time.
Required for bonding time

Returning to Work

Get a return-to-work clearance from your provider
Your healthcare provider will need to confirm you are medically cleared to return to your duties.
Before your return date

Your employer's policies

Birthing parents can take pre-birth disability leave with a doctor's note. This leave is job-protected and may be covered under your Short-Term Disability plan at 60% pay.
Birthing parents can take 6 weeks of disability for a vaginal delivery and 8 weeks of disability for a C-section delivery. This runs concurrently with FMLA and is paid at 60% through STD, topped up to 100% by your employer for the first 6 weeks.
All new parents are entitled to up to 12 weeks of bonding time. This leave is job-protected under FMLA. Your employer provides bonding time as unpaid, but it may run concurrently with any remaining FMLA entitlement.

Absence Request

Ready to make it official?

You’ve reviewed your coverage and built your plan. Now we’ll collect a few details to submit your formal absence request.

Confirm your details
Verify your employment info, dates, and leave type.
Upload any required documents
Medical certifications or supporting paperwork, if applicable.
Review & submit
You’ll see a full summary before anything is submitted.
Your plan has been saved and won’t be lost.

Select Reason For Absence Select the primary reason for your absence. Don’t worry if you’re not sure about the details yet — we’ll walk through everything together.

Employee’s Own Illness or Injury

You need time off for surgery, illness, injury, or recovery.

Care for Sick Family Member

Someone close to you needs your care due to a serious health condition.

Non-Birthing Parent, Adoption, or Foster Care

You’re welcoming a child through adoption, foster care, or as a non-birthing parent.

Birthing Parent, Pregnancy, or Bonding

You’re having a baby or need time for pregnancy and bonding.

Military Related Activity

You need time off for qualifying military exigency or to care for a service member.

Other

Your reason doesn’t fit the categories above.
This helps us identify which benefits and protections may apply to you.

Your employment & absence details

We use this to determine your eligibility for FMLA, Short-Term Disability, and state-specific benefits.

~8 weeks

Why we ask
Your work state, tenure, and absence duration determine which federal and state protections apply — including FMLA eligibility (12+ months tenure), STD coverage, and state-specific paid absence programs.

Your employer's policies

FMLA provides up to 12 weeks of job-protected, unpaid absence per year for qualifying reasons (serious health condition, family care, or new child). You must have worked 12+ months and 1,250+ hours. Your position (or equivalent) is guaranteed upon return.
If enrolled, STD provides income replacement at 60% of your base salary for up to 26 weeks after a 7-day waiting period. Coverage applies to your own medical condition only — not family care. Medical certification from your provider is required.
If you need additional time beyond your FMLA entitlement, your employer may grant extended leave as a reasonable accommodation under the ADA. This leave is unpaid and not automatically job-protected.
Pre-birth disability leave covers the period before your due date if you are medically unable to work. Typically 4 weeks, this must be certified by your medical provider. Coverage is through STD insurance at 60% of base salary, and the leave is FMLA-protected.
Post-birth disability covers your physical recovery after delivery. Vaginal delivery: 6 weeks. C-section: 8 weeks. Paid through STD at 60% of base salary after a 7-day waiting period. This leave runs concurrently with FMLA.
Bonding leave allows you up to 12 weeks to bond with your new child. This is FMLA-protected but typically unpaid unless your state offers Paid Family Leave (PFL). Bonding leave begins after your disability recovery period ends.

Your situation

This information helps us determine your coverage. All details are confidential.

Return from Absence

Select the absence you're returning from.

Welcome Back

We're glad you're preparing to return. Here's what to expect so you can be ready before confirming your return to work.

What you'll need to complete

Three quick steps — you can save your progress and come back anytime.

1
Confirm your return date
Verify or update when you plan to come back to work.
2
Set your work schedule
Choose to return full-time or ease back in with a gradual schedule.
3
Medical clearance & accommodations
Provide clearance documents if needed and request any workplace adjustments.
Once confirmed, your manager and absence administrator will be notified of your return plan.
